6de7ac50-cde3-496c-a259-8d295bd72b83.umbrellaDeptName: US Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)
6de7ac50-cde3-496c-a259-8d295bd72b83.scopeNote: FDA is a sub-agency of HHS.

b32f63f0-56e3-40e4-a086-2c356fb10441.umbrellaDeptName: US Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)
b32f63f0-56e3-40e4-a086-2c356fb10441.scopeNote: CDC is a sub-agency of HHS, the same parent department as FDA.

51606a33-de06-44d9-b679-15f558c49009.scopeNote: FSIS is an agency of the US Department of Agriculture.

7e57a953-0bad-4b44-b8c3-ccd7511ccfb6.scopeNote: AMS is an agency of the US Department of Agriculture, the same parent department as FSIS.

c07a4bb5-efac-4b15-878b-b215ff85ada0.scopeNote: TTB is a bureau of the US Department of the Treasury, reflecting its tax-and-trade (not food-safety) statutory basis.

b3769841-d7dd-4c1b-ae19-608e76bd7f14.scopeNote: OSHA is an agency of the US Department of Labor.

230c46b2-2f28-447d-bcca-93eeda95aa58.scopeNote: CBP is an agency of the US Department of Homeland Security.

1d9e799e-43ff-4d9d-bb9e-87ceb3d0d989.splitAxis: product type (shell eggs vs. egg products)
1d9e799e-43ff-4d9d-bb9e-87ceb3d0d989.splitDetail: FDA regulates intact shell eggs sold in the carton; FSIS regulates liquid, frozen, and dried egg products once the eggs are broken at a breaking plant.
1d9e799e-43ff-4d9d-bb9e-87ceb3d0d989.scopeNote: Egg regulation splits by product form rather than by a single agency's mission: shell eggs stay with FDA, but jurisdiction shifts to FSIS under the Egg Products Inspection Act the moment eggs are broken into egg products.

9d832669-ef10-445a-b786-8f319230051f.splitAxis: alcohol by volume (ABV) and formulation (malted barley + hops)
9d832669-ef10-445a-b786-8f319230051f.splitDetail: TTB regulates beer brewed with both malted barley and hops, wine at 7% ABV or greater, and distilled spirits; FDA regulates wine under 7% ABV and fermented malt beverages made without both malted barley and hops.
9d832669-ef10-445a-b786-8f319230051f.scopeNote: Beverage-alcohol jurisdiction is split by a specific statutory threshold rather than by beverage category: two nominally similar drinks can fall to two different federal agencies depending on ABV and formulation.

b6bb687a-4213-4069-bcdf-b789879ecc26.splitDetail: FDA regulates what appears on the food label and packaging itself; FTC regulates advertising and marketing claims made elsewhere (TV, print, digital, social media).
b6bb687a-4213-4069-bcdf-b789879ecc26.scopeNote: The same claim can be regulated by two different agencies depending only on where it's made: on the package (FDA) versus in an ad (FTC), per a liaison agreement dating to the 1970s.

2b90bf3d-ed9a-4230-85e0-e100c7f871fd.scopeNote: EPA sets maximum pesticide residue tolerances for food under the FD&C Act; FDA enforces those tolerances during its own inspection and sampling program rather than EPA policing residues directly.

cf1deacf-30b3-4451-87e3-969aac05c630.scopeNote: EPA sets maximum pesticide residue tolerances for meat and poultry the same way it does for FDA-regulated food; FSIS enforces those tolerances during its own inspection and sampling of meat and poultry products.

09391a88-5ee5-47db-8cf0-a471d0794610.scopeNote: CBP administers entry and customs processing for imported food shipments but refers each entry to FDA for the actual admissibility decision — CBP holds the physical/legal levers (detain, hold, refuse release) but does not decide food-safety compliance itself.

1993067e-aa1a-414a-b73e-3487c65ec509.scopeNote: FDA writes the Food Code as a model for retail/restaurant food safety, but has no authority to enforce it directly at a diner — enforcement is carried out by state and local health departments that have adopted it, in some form, in ~97% of states/territories.

6aaad084-5652-484d-a538-77aa4b494077.scopeNote: OSHA and FDA both inspect the same physical plant for different purposes: OSHA for worker safety and health conditions, FDA for product safety — neither agency defers to the other, and a facility answers to both independently and simultaneously.

62af2d47-1d13-4e9f-a823-4e8921cce336.scopeNote: OSHA and FSIS both inspect the same meat/poultry plant for different purposes: OSHA for worker safety, FSIS for product safety — this is unaffected by FSIS's continuous-presence inspection model, since the two agencies' inspectors answer to entirely separate statutory mandates.

0a741e45-6de2-4456-aa59-8033a947a313.scopeNote: States are generally free to set food-safety and labeling requirements stricter than federal FDA law (subject to federal preemption limits), so a facility can simultaneously answer to FDA at the federal level and to a stricter state or local requirement layered on top.

c079f527-10d8-4e93-b22b-51b9e35d28c6.scopeNote: State and local agencies layer their own licensing and inspection requirements on top of FSIS's federal meat/poultry oversight for anything sold direct-to-consumer at retail or produced under an intrastate-only license.
